skill.name: git-commit
skill.description: Guide for breaking changes into logical, atomic commits using interactive staging. Use when committing changes that span multiple concerns, when needing to stage parts of files (hunks), when asked to create well-organized commit history, or when changes should be split into multiple commits.
skill.summary: Organizes repository changes into logical Git commits with interactive staging guidance.
skill.author: Chemiseblanc

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown backticks and commit-related wording, but review found documentation for standard Git commands, not executable scripts or cryptographic code. The skill can affect repository history when followed, so users should review staged changes before committing.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Git Command Examples
low_findings.description: The flagged shell backticks are Markdown code fences and inline examples for Git status, diff, add, reset, and log commands. They are expected for this workflow skill, but they can modify staging state when followed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains documentation examples, not executable scripts. Commands are standard Git operations with no variable interpolation, networking, or hidden execution.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections are caused by commit-related wording in the skill description and Conventional Commits guidance. No hash algorithm, encryption routine, or crypto API appears in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines discuss Git commits and commit message format. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, encryption, hashing APIs, or security-sensitive crypto behavior.

content.user_title: Organize Git Changes into Atomic Commits
content.value_statement: Mixed changes make review and rollback harder. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through staging focused commits from one worktree.
actual_capabilities: Reviews changed files with Git status and diff commands.
Identifies natural commit boundaries by feature, fix, refactor, or configuration change.
Guides whole-file staging and interactive hunk staging.
Encourages verification of staged changes before each commit.
Supports repeated commits until the worktree is organized.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not guarantee perfect commit boundaries.
It requires a Git repository with local changes to be useful.
It does not define full commit message rules by itself.
It can change staging state when its Git commands are followed.

use_cases.title: Split a mixed feature branch
use_cases.description: Separate unrelated feature, bug fix, and cleanup changes into reviewable commits.

use_cases.title: Prepare a clean pull request
use_cases.description: Stage and verify each logical change before opening a pull request.

use_cases.title: Preserve review feedback history
use_cases.description: Group changes from review comments into commits that can be inspected and reverted independently.

prompt_templates.title: Review my changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the git-commit skill to inspect my current changes and suggest logical commit groups.

prompt_templates.title: Stage the first commit
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the git-commit skill to stage only the files for the first logical commit, then show me the staged diff summary.

prompt_templates.title: Split changes inside files
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the git-commit skill to separate unrelated hunks in the same files with interactive staging.

prompt_templates.title: Create a full commit sequence
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the git-commit skill to organize all current changes into a sequence of atomic commits and verify each one.

output_examples.input: I changed validation, styles, and documentation in one branch.
output: Commit 1: validation behavior with related tests.

output_examples.input: A file contains both a bug fix and a refactor.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends interactive hunk staging, then reviewing the staged diff before committing the bug fix.

output_examples.input: I need to confirm what remains after one commit.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends checking repository status and recent commit history before continuing with the next logical change.

best_practices: Review the staged diff before every commit.
Keep each commit focused on one reversible concern.
Separate file moves or renames from behavior changes when possible.
anti_patterns: Do not stage all changes when the branch contains unrelated work.
Do not mix dependency updates with feature code without a clear reason.
Do not commit generated files unless they are expected project outputs.

faq.question: Does this skill create commits automatically?
faq.answer: It provides a workflow for staging and committing. The agent or user must still run Git commands.

faq.question: Can it split changes within one file?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends interactive hunk staging with Git patch mode.

faq.question: Does it write commit messages?
faq.answer: It gives basic message guidance and refers to a separate commit-message skill for detailed formatting.

faq.question: Is it safe for repositories with uncommitted work?
faq.answer: It is intended for that case, but users should review each staged diff before committing.

faq.question: Can it decide commit boundaries?
faq.answer: It helps identify boundaries by purpose, files, reversibility, and review context.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed skill only documents local Git workflow commands.
